items.myth: Feature selection always improves model accuracy.
items.reality: Removing features can hurt if you drop signal or pick subsets that overfit validation. Selection is worthwhile when it reduces noise, cost, or complexity without sacrificing held-out metrics.
items.example: Dropping half the features via MI might lower test AUC if weak-looking features interact strongly in a tree model.

items.myth: L1-selected features are the globally optimal subset.
items.reality: Lasso performs convex relaxation of subset selection; correlated features compete for one coefficient slot and the chosen set depends on λ and scaling.
items.example: Two duplicate columns may split coefficient mass so both survive weakly, or one is zeroed arbitrarily depending on noise.

items.myth: Feature selection and PCA solve the same problem.
items.reality: Selection keeps original axes; PCA builds orthogonal combinations maximizing variance. PCA can capture joint structure selection misses; selection stays interpretable.
items.example: Three correlated sensors might need one PCA component for 95% variance, but selection keeps one sensor name clinicians recognize.
